sound_type: slow deliberate drumming -- approximately 10-15 strikes/sec, slower than most Picidae. Individual strikes perceptible. Measured, methodical. Also sharp pik call. E=4.
ecosystem: Beetle-kill forest, burned forest, boreal and subalpine conifer. Follows bark beetle outbreaks across the landscape.
season: Resident (year-round). Non-migratory but nomadic -- follows beetle outbreaks to new areas.

coordinates.description: Reference for subalpine/boreal forest habitat in the North Cascades. Three-toed Woodpeckers found in beetle-kill and burned areas at higher elevations.

taxonomy_note.description: Eighth consecutive Picidae (107-114). BREAKS the all-time family-streak record (previous: Corvidae septet 100-106, seven). Picoides dorsalis -- genus Picoides, distinct from the Dryobates (110-111, 113), Melanerpes (107, 112), and Dryocopus (108-109) genera in the streak. Four genera across eight degrees. Three toes instead of four -- wider swing arc for bark flaking. Beetle-kill specialist. Yellow crown (males). E=4.
taxonomy_note.current_family: Picidae (8th consecutive, 11th entry total -- NEW RECORD)
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Picidae count: 11 entries (Northern Flicker 21-22, Red-breasted Sapsucker 54, Pileated Woodpecker 54, Acorn Woodpecker 107, Pileated Woodpecker laughing 108, Pileated Woodpecker drumming 109, Hairy Woodpecker 110, Downy Woodpecker 111, Lewis's Woodpecker 112, White-headed Woodpecker 113, Three-toed Woodpecker 114)
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Picoides from Latin picus ('woodpecker') + Greek -oides ('resembling') -- 'resembling a woodpecker.' dorsalis from Latin 'of the back,' referring to the barred back pattern. The 'woodpecker-like bird with a barred back.'
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Picoides -- distinct from Dryobates (Hairy 110, Downy 111, White-headed 113), Melanerpes (Acorn 107, Lewis's 112), and Dryocopus (Pileated 108-109). Fourth genus in the Picidae octet. Formerly included the Eurasian Three-toed Woodpecker (P. tridactylus), split in 2003.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Piciformes. Eighth consecutive (107-114). NEW ALL-TIME FAMILY-STREAK RECORD. Surpasses Corvidae septet (100-106, seven).
taxonomy_note.family_position: Picidae octet (107-114) is the new longest family streak in the engine. Eight consecutive degrees. BREAKS the Corvidae record by one.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: 500-2,000 Hz. Low-to-mid range, depending on resonant properties of drumming substrate (dead wood).
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 800-1,500 Hz. Low-frequency drumming on dead wood.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Drumming: 500-2,000 Hz, slow and deliberate. Pik call: 3,000-5,000 Hz, sharp and clipped.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Drumming: 0.5-1.5 seconds per bout. Pik call: very brief, approximately 0.1 seconds.
song_metrics.note_structure: DRUMMING: Slow, deliberate series of strikes. Individual strikes more perceptible than in faster-drumming species. Approximately 10-15 strikes/second. PIK CALL: Single sharp note.
song_metrics.note_types: Slow deliberate drum (primary territorial/communication), sharp pik call (contact/agitation). The drum is more diagnostic than the call.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Drum: even, measured roll. No acceleration or deceleration within the bout. Consistent tempo.
song_metrics.timbre: Drumming: Resonant, measured, each strike distinct. Dead wood amplifies each hit. Not thunderous (like Pileated) or sharp (like Hairy). Measured and methodical. Pik call: sharp, similar to Hairy's peek! but softer.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=4 -- MODERATE energy. Slow drumming is not loud or carrying. Bark flaking is relatively quiet. A quiet specialist in quiet dead forests.
song_metrics.tempo: SLOW. Approximately 10-15 strikes/sec (Hairy ~25/sec, Pileated ~15-20/sec). The slowest drummer in the Picidae streak.
song_metrics.context: TERRITORIAL AND COMMUNICATION. Drumming used for territory establishment and mate communication. Pik call for contact. Less acoustically active than most woodpeckers.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Minimal. Drumming tempo consistent across range.
song_metrics.key_property: SLOW DELIBERATE DRUMMING -- ~10-15 strikes/sec, individual strikes perceptible. E=4. THREE TOES -- wider swing arc for bark flaking. BEETLE-KILL SPECIALIST -- follows bark beetle outbreaks. BREAKS ALL-TIME RECORD -- 8th consecutive Picidae (107-114). Yellow crown (males). Fourth genus in octet (Picoides).

plumage.overall: Medium-sized woodpecker. Black-and-white. Black head with white facial stripes (moustachial and postocular). Black wings with white barring. White back with black barring. Flanks barred black-and-white. Males: YELLOW crown patch (diagnostic -- not red). Females: no crown patch.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: For degree 114: (1) YELLOW CROWN PATCH in males (unique among streak species). (2) WHITE BARRED BACK visible on trunk. (3) Black-and-white pattern without red markings. (4) THREE-TOED FEET (field diagnostic if seen). (5) Beetle-kill/burned forest habitat context.
plumage.flight_pattern: Undulating (typical woodpecker). White back barring visible in flight.

physical_description.overall: Medium-sized woodpecker. Body length 21-23 cm. Wingspan 37-39 cm. Weight 55-70 g. Black-and-white with barred back. Slightly stockier than Hairy Woodpecker.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 114: black-and-white woodpecker with yellow crown (males) in beetle-kill or burned forest. Three toes (2 forward, 1 back) if foot visible. Barred white back. Slow deliberate drumming.
physical_description.field_id: Yellow-crowned (males) black-and-white woodpecker in dead or beetle-kill forest. Bark flaking on standing dead trees. Slow drumming. White barred back. Three toes.

nodes.title: BREAKS All-Time Record -- 8th Consecutive Picidae
nodes.description: Picidae octet (107-114) surpasses Corvidae septet (100-106, seven). Eight degrees, four genera. From social to solitary, generalist to specialist, healthy forest to disturbed. The most comprehensive family survey in the engine. New all-time record.

nodes.title: Beetle-Kill Specialist -- Following Bark Beetle Outbreaks
nodes.description: Primary vertebrate predator of bark beetle larvae. Concentrates in beetle-kill areas in numbers far exceeding normal density. Follows outbreaks across the landscape. Natural biocontrol agent. Climate change intensifying outbreaks -- more food short-term, ecosystem risk long-term.

nodes.title: Three-Toed Foot -- Biomechanical Optimization
nodes.description: Three toes (2 forward, 1 back) instead of standard four. Missing rear inner toe. Wider swing arc for more powerful bark flaking. Less stability traded for more force. The bird has reduced its own anatomy to improve its primary function. Less is more. Three toes do what four cannot.

nodes.title: Disturbance Ecology Trio (112-114)
nodes.description: Three consecutive disturbance-dependent species: Lewis's (112, burned forest), White-headed (113, fire-maintained ponderosa), Three-toed (114, beetle-kill). The Picidae streak's ecological narrative: from healthy forest to disturbed forest. From stability to disruption. From trees alive to trees dead.

nodes.title: Slow Deliberate Drumming -- Optimized for Dead Wood
nodes.description: Approximately 10-15 strikes/sec (Hairy ~25/sec). Individual strikes perceptible. Methodical, measured. May be optimized for dead wood resonance -- slower strikes on lighter dead wood produce louder, more resonant sound. The slowest drummer in the Picidae streak. E=4.

nodes.title: Genus Picoides -- Fourth Genus in the Octet
nodes.description: Picoides is the fourth genus in the Picidae octet: Melanerpes (107, 112), Dryocopus (108-109), Dryobates (110-111, 113), Picoides (114). Four genera across eight degrees. Formerly included Eurasian Three-toed (split 2003). Yellow crown in males (not red) is genus-diagnostic.

cross_references.relationship: BARK-FLAKING SPECIALISTS. Two consecutive bark flakers (113-114). White-headed pries loose bark on ponderosa. Three-toed flakes bark on beetle-kill with wider swing arc (three toes). Both E=4.
DISTURBANCE ECOLOGY TRIO. Three consecutive disturbance-dependent (112-114). Lewis's: burned forest. White-headed: fire-maintained. Three-toed: beetle-kill. From healthy to disturbed.
FORAGING CONTRAST. Hairy: generalist driller, 4 toes (E=4). Three-toed: specialist bark flaker, 3 toes (E=4). Same energy, different strategies. The generalist-specialist trade-off.
SIZE/POWER CONTRAST. Pileated: largest, deepest drilling, thunderous drum (E=6). Three-toed: medium, bark flaking, slow deliberate drum (E=4). Extremes of the Picidae octet.
PICIDAE OCTET ENDPOINTS. Acorn at start (107), Three-toed breaks record (114). Colonial/social vs. solitary/specialist. Eight-degree journey from first to record-breaking entry.
